players.name: Matt Turner
players.bio: The Revolution's reliable last line of defence, Turner earned his stripes as Arsenal's backup before returning stateside. A shot-stopper with 54 caps, he's the veteran presence between the posts for the Stars and Stripes on home soil.
players.facts: Turner worked as a youth soccer coach before breaking into professional football — a proper late bloomer who peaked at just the right time.

players.name: Sergiño Dest
players.bio: Born in the Netherlands to an American father, Dest chose the Stars and Stripes over the Oranje — a decision that's paid dividends. The dynamic right-back dazzles with pace and trickery, equally at home bombing forward or tracking back.
players.facts: Dest was courted by both the USA and Netherlands national teams — he picked the Americans, and the Dutch have been crying into their tulips ever since.

players.name: Chris Richards
players.bio: The Birmingham-born centre-back has made Crystal Palace's backline his home, bringing composure and quality in the air. A product of FC Dallas's academy, Richards has quietly become one of the USMNT's most dependable defenders.
players.facts: Richards was born in Birmingham, Alabama — making him a true son of the American South defending the red, white, and blue.

players.name: Tyler Adams
players.bio: The Bournemouth dynamo is the engine room of the USMNT midfield — press-resistant, tenacious, and born on Valentine's Day. Adams captains with authority beyond his years and sets the tempo wherever he plays.
players.facts: Adams was born on 14 February — so every time the USMNT wins, it's a Valentine's gift to the nation. Romantic stuff.

players.name: Antonee Robinson
players.bio: Nicknamed 'Jedi', Robinson is one of the most marauding left-backs in world football. The Fulham stalwart brings blistering pace and a surprising eye for goal to the USMNT, lighting up the left flank game after game.
players.facts: Robinson was born in Milton Keynes, England — making him one of several USMNT players who could theoretically have played for the Three Lions.

players.name: Auston Trusty
players.bio: The Philadelphia-born defender found his calling in the Scottish Premiership with Celtic, winning titles and earning plaudits. A no-nonsense centre-back with good feet, Trusty is living proof that trust is earned, not given.
players.facts: Trusty won the Scottish Premiership title with Celtic — not many USMNT players can say they've lifted silverware in Glasgow.

players.name: Giovanni Reyna
players.bio: Son of USMNT legend Claudio Reyna, Gio is the crown jewel of the golden generation. A technically gifted attacking midfielder, his World Cup 2026 arrival on home soil feels like destiny after injury frustrations in Qatar 2022.
players.facts: Reyna's father Claudio and mother Danielle Egan both played for the USMNT and USWNT respectively — football royalty, plain and simple.

players.name: Weston McKennie
players.bio: The little Texan with the big personality, McKennie has become a Champions League-era regular at Juventus. An all-action box-to-box midfielder who chips in with crucial goals, Weston is the heartbeat of the USMNT engine room.
players.facts: McKennie once attended a Juventus team meeting via Zoom while on holiday — his coach wasn't amused. He was reportedly fined for it.

players.name: Ricardo Pepi
players.bio: El Paso's finest striker, Pepi burst onto the scene with FC Dallas before making his mark in the Eredivisie with PSV. A physical and clinical centre-forward with 13 international goals, he's the fox in the box the USMNT have long craved.
players.facts: Pepi's family is of Mexican descent — making his goals against Mexico in World Cup qualifying particularly spicy viewing for all involved.

players.name: Christian Pulisic
players.bio: The Captain America. Born in Hershey, Pennsylvania — the chocolate capital of the US — Pulisic is anything but sweet on defenders. The Milan star is the USMNT's all-time great of his generation, with 33 goals and the world at his feet.
players.facts: Pulisic was born in Hershey, PA — home of Hershey's chocolate. His goals for the USMNT are, admittedly, even sweeter.

players.name: Brenden Aaronson
players.bio: The high-energy New Jersey kid who never stops running, Aaronson made his name at Red Bull Salzburg before returning to Leeds United. His infectious pressing game and knack for big moments make him a fan favourite wherever he plays.
players.facts: Aaronson scored a crucial World Cup qualifying goal against Mexico in Columbus — a night that's already become USMNT folklore.

players.name: Miles Robinson
players.bio: A commanding presence at the heart of FC Cincinnati's defence, Miles Robinson has been one of MLS's finest centre-backs. After recovering from a serious Achilles injury, his World Cup 2026 place represents a remarkable comeback story.
players.facts: Robinson ruptured his Achilles tendon in 2022, missing the Qatar World Cup — making his 2026 selection a genuine against-the-odds redemption arc.

players.name: Tim Ream
players.bio: The elder statesman of the USMNT backline, Ream is the coolest head in the room at 38 years old. A St. Louis native who built his career in England, the Charlotte FC captain brings leadership and calmness that money simply cannot buy.
players.facts: Ream is the oldest player in the USMNT squad — at 38, he was already 11 years old when some of his teammates were born.

players.name: Sebastian Berhalter
players.bio: Son of former USMNT coach Gregg Berhalter, Sebastian has carved his own path at Vancouver Whitecaps. The London-born central midfielder brings intelligence and technical quality, proving the football gene runs deep in the Berhalter family.
players.facts: Sebastian was coached at international level by his own father, Gregg Berhalter — a dynamic that requires some serious dinner-table diplomacy.

players.name: Cristian Roldan
players.bio: A Seattle Sounders institution, Roldan has been one of MLS's most consistent midfielders for nearly a decade. His tireless work rate and ability to win the ball back make him an invaluable squad option for the USMNT on home turf.
players.facts: Roldan has spent his entire professional career at Seattle Sounders — a rare one-club man in the modern era of football's revolving door.

players.name: Alex Freeman
players.bio: Fort Lauderdale's brightest export, Freeman is the exciting young right-back who caught Villarreal's eye with electric performances. At just 21, he arrives at his first World Cup as one of the USMNT's most thrilling prospects going forward.
players.facts: Freeman is one of the youngest players in the USMNT squad — at 21, the World Cup on home soil could be his coming-of-age moment.

players.name: Malik Tillman
players.bio: Born in Nuremberg, Tillman was raised in Germany before choosing the Stars and Stripes — and Bayer Leverkusen's title-winning culture has sharpened his game considerably. A clever, technical attacking midfielder with an eye for goal.
players.facts: Tillman was part of Bayer Leverkusen's historic unbeaten Bundesliga title-winning squad in 2023-24 — the first time Leverkusen ever won the German top flight.

players.name: Max Arfsten
players.bio: The Columbus Crew's composed full-back, Arfsten brings solidity and athleticism to the USMNT defensive options. Still building his international reputation, the 25-year-old is a reliable squad member trusted by the coaching staff.
players.facts: Arfsten plays for Columbus Crew — one of MLS's founding clubs, meaning he represents genuine American football heritage.

players.name: Haji Wright
players.bio: The journeyman striker who saved the USMNT in Qatar with a stunning backheel flick against Iran. Wright's unconventional career path — from LA Galaxy to Coventry — makes him one of football's most entertaining characters.
players.facts: Wright's backheel goal against Iran at World Cup 2022 is one of the most audacious finishes in USMNT history. Accidental genius or pure class? Debate rages on.

players.name: Folarin Balogun
players.bio: London-born and New York-raised, Balogun chose the USMNT over England and Nigeria — and has never looked back. The Monaco striker's clinical finishing and intelligent movement make him a constant threat in the final third.
players.facts: Balogun was eligible for England, Nigeria, and the USA — choosing the Stars and Stripes after a careful international tug-of-war few players ever experience.

players.name: Timothy Weah
players.bio: Son of Liberian legend and former FIFA World Player of the Year George Weah, Timothy carries serious footballing DNA. The Marseille winger has pace to burn and a nose for big moments, representing the USMNT with pride and pedigree.
players.facts: Timothy's father, George Weah, won the 1995 Ballon d'Or and later became President of Liberia — making Timothy's family dinner conversations rather extraordinary.

players.name: Mark McKenzie
players.bio: The Toulouse centre-back has steadily built a solid international career across 29 caps. McKenzie's reading of the game and composure on the ball make him a trustworthy defensive option as the USMNT navigate their home World Cup.
players.facts: McKenzie plays his club football in France's Ligue 1 with Toulouse — one of several USMNT defenders scattered impressively across Europe's top leagues.

players.name: Joe Scally
players.bio: The Long Island kid who made Germany his footballing home, Scally has established himself as one of Borussia Mönchengladbach's first-choice full-backs. Technically assured and tactically savvy, he's a modern right-back in every sense.
players.facts: Scally was born on 31 December 2002 — meaning he's spent every New Year's Eve of his life also celebrating his birthday. The ultimate party trick.

players.name: Matt Freese
players.bio: New York City FC's dependable shot-stopper, Freese has quietly accumulated 15 caps as the USMNT's experienced backup goalkeeper. Composed under pressure and a commanding presence in his box, he's a trusted understudy to Turner.
players.facts: Freese studied at Penn State University before turning professional — one of the USMNT goalkeepers who took the college soccer route to the big stage.

players.name: Chris Brady
players.bio: The youngest goalkeeper in the squad, Brady is already making waves at Chicago Fire despite being just 22. With a single senior cap to his name, World Cup 2026 represents the beginning of what could be a very long USMNT story.
players.facts: Brady was born on 3 March 2004 — meaning he was still in primary school when some of his USMNT teammates were already turning professional.

players.name: Alejandro Zendejas
players.bio: Born in the USA but raised in Mexico's football culture, Zendejas plays his club football at Club América and chose the Stars and Stripes. A tricky, direct winger who adds genuine depth to the USMNT's attacking options from the flanks.
players.facts: Zendejas plays for Club América in Mexico — making him the only USMNT squad member whose club fans might have very mixed feelings about cheering him on.
